name: Rossville Road Cut
short_description: Roadside exposure near Warrington Township offering access to Precambrian gneiss and schist outcrops. Look for quartz, feldspar, and mica crystals in this classic Pennsylvania metamorphic terrain.
description:
Rossville Road Cut is a natural roadside exposure that reveals the Precambrian bedrock characteristic of the Pennsylvania Piedmont province. The outcrop displays metamorphic rocks including gneiss and schist that were formed during intense mountain-building events over 400 million years ago. This is an accessible location for collectors interested in studying metamorphic mineral assemblages and collecting quartz and feldspar specimens.

The road cut provides excellent views of foliated metamorphic rocks with visible mineral layers. Mica sheets, quartz veins, and feldspar crystals are commonly visible in the exposed bedrock. The relatively flat terrain and roadside access make this site popular with both casual collectors and serious rockhounds studying the regional geology.

Because this is a road cut exposure, the collected material is often loose or easily detached from weathered surfaces. The site offers good educational value for understanding Pennsylvania's ancient geological history and the types of minerals found in Piedmont metamorphic rocks. Best collecting occurs after seasonal weathering has loosened additional material.
history: This outcrop exposes rocks from the Precambrian basement of the Pennsylvania Piedmont, metamorphosed during the Ordovician Taconic orogeny when continental plates collided. The road cut itself was created during modern road construction, revealing fresh metamorphic rock faces that have been yielding specimens to collectors for decades.
tips: Search the base and lower portions of the road cut where weathered material has accumulated - this is where loose specimens are most abundant. Bring a small chisel or rock hammer to carefully remove specimens from friable seams within the schist layers. Look for areas where quartz veins cross-cut the darker metamorphic host rock, as these zones often contain the best-formed crystals and feldspar pockets.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 618 feet elevation. Roadside location with direct access from the road shoulder. Walking surface is rocky with loose material and weathered fragments scattered at the base of the cut. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp metamorphic rock fragments.
best_season: April - June, September - November. Spring and fall temperatures are ideal for roadside collecting. Summer heat can be intense on exposed road cuts, and winter weather may limit safe access to the road shoulder.
written_directions: From York, Pennsylvania, travel north on PA-261 toward Warrington Township. The Rossville Road Cut is located along local roads in the Warrington area at the coordinates provided. Look for exposed metamorphic rock faces along the roadside. Parking is available on road shoulders near the exposure - use caution and observe all traffic laws when stopped.
road_conditions: Paved road with shoulder parking. Direct roadside access to the exposure.
parking_notes: Informal roadside parking on shoulders near the road cut. Limited space - be considerate of traffic and park safely away from the traveled portion of the road.
collection_rules: Collect only loose material and weathered fragments from the base of the road cut. Do not hammer on the outcrop face itself or attempt to enlarge the exposure. Respect private property adjacent to the road cut. Use only hand tools. Keep collections to reasonable personal use quantities.
rules:
Collect only loose material and fragments
No hammering on active outcrop faces
Use hand tools only
Respect adjacent private property
Observe all traffic safety rules while parked
Personal use only - no commercial collection
nearest_services: York, Pennsylvania - approximately 8 miles south. Full services including gas, food, and supplies available in York.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most visitors. Roadside location with short walking distance from parking. Rocky footing at the base of the cut requires sturdy footwear. No wheelchair accessibility due to roadside terrain and loose rock fragments.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Rossville Road Cut in Pennsylvania?
faq.answer: Rossville Road Cut exposes Precambrian metamorphic rocks containing quartz, feldspar, and mica. The quartz appears in veins cross-cutting the gneiss and schist layers, while feldspar crystals are visible throughout the metamorphic host rock. Mica sheets are abundant and easily collected from weathered material at the base of the cut.

faq.question: Is Rossville Road Cut free to collect at and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Rossville Road Cut is free to access and no permit is required. It is public roadside property where collectors can gather loose material and weathered fragments from the base of the road cut without any fees or special permission.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Rossville Road Cut?
faq.answer: The best seasons are April through June and September through November. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures ideal for spending time at an exposed road cut. Summer heat can be intense, and winter weather may limit safe access to the roadside location.

faq.question: How do you find good specimens at this Pennsylvania metamorphic rock outcrop?
faq.answer: Focus your search on the base and lower portions of the road cut where weathered material has naturally loosened and fallen. Look for areas where lighter quartz veins intersect darker schist layers - these zones often contain the best-formed crystals. Bring a small chisel to carefully separate specimens from friable seams.

faq.question: Is Rossville Road Cut in York County good for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Rossville Road Cut is excellent for beginners. The roadside location requires no hiking, parking is simple, and most specimens are loose or easily detached. It offers great educational value for learning about metamorphic rocks and Pennsylvania geology with minimal physical demands.

faq.question: What tools should you bring when rockhounding at Rossville Road Cut?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel for carefully removing specimens from weathered seams, a collection bag for specimens, sturdy hiking boots or work shoes due to sharp rock fragments, and work gloves for protection. A magnifying glass is helpful for examining feldspar and mica crystals. Avoid large hammers or power tools - only hand tools are appropriate at this roadside exposure.

meta_title: Rossville Road Cut Rockhounding - York County PA
meta_description: Free roadside rockhounding near York, PA. Find quartz, feldspar, and mica in Precambrian metamorphic rocks. Beginner-friendly, no permit needed.
